title: What separates firms that use AI intensively from firms that don’t?
gist: ECB survey data (SAFE, Q4 2025) show over 70% of euro area firms now use AI but only 7% use it intensively, and the gap between the two groups is explained by firm size, sector, peer-pressure dynamics, and access to broader (especially bank-loan) financing rather than by AI adoption itself.

insights.claim: More than 70% of euro area firms reported using AI in the last quarter of 2025, but only 7% reported using it intensively.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Headline AI-adoption statistics vastly overstate the share of firms positioned to capture productivity gains; investors should discount broad 'AI adoption' figures and look for intensity metrics instead.
insights.quote: more than 70% of firms reported using AI (Chart 1, panel a), a trend that looks set to continue... only 7% of euro area firms reporting intensive use
insights.locator: AI diffusion continues to rise, but deepening takes time

insights.claim: Nearly half of the euro area firms that were not using AI in 2025 plan to invest in it in 2026, meaning adoption breadth will keep rising even though intensity remains rare.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A further wave of AI adoption is coming in 2026, but based on the article's own logic this will mostly add shallow/peripheral users rather than intensive ones, capping near-term productivity impact.
insights.locator: AI diffusion continues to rise, but deepening takes time

insights.claim: Simply adopting AI does not guarantee measurable efficiency gains; only intensive use tied to core processes, innovation and expansion of products and services is likely to boost productivity and support growth.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Macro forecasts and equity narratives that equate AI adoption rates with productivity upside are likely overstating near-term impact; the real signal is what share of a firm's AI use touches core processes.
insights.quote: Firms that apply AI to core processes tend to generate more value than those that restrict its use to peripheral or routine tasks.

insights.claim: Among firms that already use AI, intensive use is relatively more common among small firms than large ones, even though overall AI adoption likelihood rises with firm size.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Investors screening for AI-driven productivity winners should not default to large-cap 'AI adopters' as the intensity leaders — smaller, more agile adopters may be pound-for-pound more transformed.
insights.locator: AI diffusion continues to rise, but deepening takes time

insights.claim: Younger firms (active less than ten years) report intensive AI use more often than older firms, but firm size is more closely associated with intensive use than firm age overall.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Size, not youth, is the dominant screening variable for identifying which firms will deepen AI use — useful for building a firm-level AI-intensity factor.
insights.locator: AI diffusion continues to rise, but deepening takes time

insights.claim: Intensive AI use is particularly prevalent in high-tech, knowledge-intensive services such as information and communication (ICT), because these firms are highly digitalised, have abundant data and computing infrastructure, and employ technically skilled workers.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: ICT/tech-services names are the natural first movers to watch for realized AI productivity gains, since the preconditions (data, compute, skills) are already in place.
insights.locator: AI diffusion continues to rise, but deepening takes time

themes.theme: Motivations shift as firms move up the intensity curve

insights.claim: Firms at an early stage of AI adoption cite cost reduction and operational efficiency as their main reasons for using it.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Early-stage AI spend is defensive/cost-focused, implying limited top-line upside from that cohort in the near term.
insights.locator: Why do firms use AI? The reasons differ

insights.claim: Intensive AI users are more frequently motivated by growth and innovation, citing employment growth, support for research and development, and expansion of products and services as key reasons for adopting AI.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Intensive AI use correlates with hiring and R&D expansion rather than headcount cuts, a useful counter to the narrative that heavier AI use mainly displaces labor.
insights.locator: Why do firms use AI? The reasons differ

themes.theme: Peer pressure as a hidden driver of intensity

insights.claim: A 10 percentage point increase in the current share of peers investing in AI raises the probability that a firm is an intensive AI user by about 1.9 percentage points, while a 10-point increase in the expected future peer AI-investment rate raises it by about 1.4 percentage points.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: AI intensification spreads through competitive contagion within a sector/size cohort, not just organic ROI calculations — useful for modeling diffusion speed sector by sector.
insights.quote: A 10 percentage point increase in the current (future) AI investment rate increases the probability that a firm is AI-intensive by about 1.9 (1.4) percentage points.
insights.locator: Intensive use driven by peer pressure

insights.claim: The peer-pressure effect on intensive AI use is driven primarily by incumbent, well-established firms rather than young firms, meaning incumbents intensify AI use because they feel threatened by technologically advanced young firms and high-performing peers.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Incumbents in sectors with rising young-firm/high-growth-firm density are the ones most likely to accelerate AI capex defensively — a leading indicator for capex surprises among legacy players.
insights.locator: Intensive use driven by peer pressure

insights.claim: The peer-pressure effect on AI intensity is most pronounced in ICT and professional services, sectors characterized by a high share of young firms, many high-growth firms, and exposure to technologically advanced foreign competitors.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Competitive-contagion-driven AI capex is concentrated in ICT and professional services; sectors like construction, manufacturing, energy, real estate and trade show no significant peer effect, so expect much slower intensity diffusion there.
insights.locator: Intensive use driven by peer pressure

themes.theme: Financing intensive AI use is structurally different

insights.claim: More than 84% of firms reporting intensive AI use have invested in the technology, versus only 33% of moderate users, and 99% of intensive users plan to invest in AI in 2026, allocating around 20% of their total investment to AI-related activities.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Intensive AI users are directing roughly a fifth of total capex to AI going forward — a concrete benchmark for gauging whether a firm's AI spend is material or token.
insights.locator: Intensive AI use requires broader financing

insights.claim: Integrating AI into core processes (customised solutions, upgraded digital infrastructure) requires larger, longer-term restructuring investment that cannot easily be financed through short-term instruments like trade credit or own funds.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Firms stuck relying on short-term/own-fund financing are structurally capped in how intensively they can deploy AI, regardless of intent — a financing-access screen doubles as an AI-intensity screen.
insights.locator: Intensive AI use requires broader financing

insights.claim: Firms using AI intensively are more likely to combine several financing sources and, because euro area firms have fewer ways to raise money directly from investors or markets, rely more heavily on bank loans to finance AI investment — in contrast to AI users generally, who rely more on own funds compared with non-users.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Bank credit conditions and euro area capital-market shallowness are a direct bottleneck on AI intensification; tighter bank lending standards would disproportionately slow the shift from moderate to intensive AI use, more so than it slows basic adoption.
insights.locator: Intensive AI use requires broader financing

insights.claim: Firms with public shareholders (listed firms) are more likely to adopt AI at all, possibly reflecting professional management's greater willingness to adopt new technology, but being publicly owned does not make a firm more likely to progress from moderate to intensive AI use.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Public listing is a marker of AI adoption but not of AI intensity — investors should not assume listed-company AI initiatives are more transformative than those at private peers.
insights.locator: Intensive AI use requires broader financing

themes.theme: Barriers to intensification and policy levers

insights.claim: The most-cited barriers to expanding AI use are shortages of AI-related skills (40% of firms), limited usefulness of current AI technologies for the firm's business needs (28%), and incompatibility with existing systems (26%).
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Skills scarcity is by far the largest bottleneck, larger than technology limitations — vendors and providers offering AI upskilling/integration services address the biggest reported pain point and stand to capture disproportionate demand.
insights.locator: What can help firms intensify their AI use?

insights.claim: The authors suggest targeted policy support — promoting the sharing of successful AI use cases, applied training programmes for managers, employees and IT specialists, and subsidised advisory services — could help small and medium-sized enterprises scale up AI use.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Watch for EU/national policy programmes on AI training and advisory subsidies aimed at SMEs; such programmes would specifically target the skills bottleneck the survey identifies as the top barrier.
insights.locator: What can help firms intensify their AI use?

top_entities: ECB Survey on the access to finance of enterprises (SAFE)
